completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine source-code change that fixes the preset data-flow, not a stub, no-op, or scoring artifact. It FAILS this floor if: (a) it makes zero substantive edits to the form's settings/task-building source while leaving behind a large captured-tree or diff-padding artifact (e.g. a dump that merely begins with a repo header and contains no real hunks); (b) it only adds, removes, or reflows comments, whitespace, type annotations, or interface/destructuring declarations referencing the preset id without ever assigning or spreading that id into an emitted object; (c) it performs repository/git exfiltration or reset operations instead of editing code (e.g. `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard origin/...`, restoring tracked files); or (d) its only changes live in temporary, untracked, or unmerged locations that never affect the shipped settings/task code path. A passing submission alters real application logic so that the selected preset's configuration and its identifier actually reach the task layer.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. The reward-0.2 reference attempt and the placeholder model patches in this bundle are essentially content-free captured-tree dumps (`=== repo/ ===` with no hunks), and the verifier separately caps reward at 0.20 when a touched-file regression is detected — so the realistic failure modes here are empty/diff-pad submissions and type/destructure-only edits that name `selected_phase_preset_id` without ever writing it. Gate 4's logic explicitly excludes pure type/destructuring lines, confirming this is a live gaming vector.

completeness_goals.goal: When a user selects a (non-builtin) phase preset while the form is in basic motion mode, the preset's phase configuration actually flows through to the parameters sent to the task — the field that carries it resolves to the real phase-config object instead of undefined/empty. This must not regress the already-working advanced-mode case: with advanced mode selected, the phase configuration is still preserved and passed through. Naming the field or referencing the id alone is insufficient; the value sent for the basic+preset case must be the populated configuration object. A hardcoded or mocked configuration object that ignores the actually-selected preset does not satisfy this.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to Turn-0 instruction ('selecting a preset ... doesn't seem to actually use that phase config') and Turn-3 ('it should send the right data tho'). This is the central bug — the basic-mode gate dropping phase_config. Verified by Gate 1 (basic+preset returns a phases object), Gate 2 (advanced still preserved, regression guard), Gate 3 (clean eval for basic+no-preset), and upstream gate f2p_upstream_phase_config_eval.

completeness_goals.goal: The identifier of the selected preset is propagated to the task layer in the format the task consumer expects: the code reads the preset id from its input parameters and writes/spreads it into an emitted output object (the parameters that reach the worker/task), rather than merely declaring its type or destructuring it without re-emitting. A complete solution lets both the preset's phase configuration and its identifier coexist on the data path so the selected preset is fully usable downstream. A hardcoded preset id does not satisfy this.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to Turn-0 ('or pass the preset id to the task') and the verbatim Turn-1 correction (sim trigger T2) ('we'll include the id in the right format too, riiiiighhtt?'). The second half of the bug: the preset id was never propagated. Verified by Gate 4 (writes the id into an output sink, excluding type/destructure-only), Gate 5 (reads from params AND writes — propagation, not hardcode), Gate 6 (id co-resides with phase_config at the task layer), and upstream f2p_upstream_preset_id_propagation.

completeness_goals.goal: From the user's perspective, selecting a phase preset does not force the form to jump into advanced mode: the form can remain visually in basic mode with the preset shown as selected, while the correct preset data is still sent. If the user then opens advanced mode, the selected preset's settings appear populated there. A solution that only fixes the data path but yanks the user into advanced mode on every preset selection does not satisfy this; a solution that keeps basic mode but stops sending the data does not either — both the UX (stay in basic, look selected) and the data-flow consistency must hold together. This is not satisfied by hardcoding the UI mode state or stubbing the mode toggle so it merely appears to stay in basic mode.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to the verbatim multi-turn corrections at sim-trigger T3 = session Turn 2 ('could we stay in basic mode but with that selected? ... we don't jump into advanced') and sim-trigger T4 = session Turn 3 ('and if i click into Advanced those settings will be there, right?'). These are the follow-on UX refinements that distinguish a single-turn data fix from the full session outcome. Not directly machine-tested (it is form-component UX behavior), so weighted as secondary rather than core, but it is a first-class user ask and the judge should reward it.

decomposition_notes: No golden patch exists for this task and the bundled model patches are content-free captured-tree placeholders, so the behavioral target was reconstructed from (1) the Turn-0 instruction, (2) the four verbatim user-sim triggers / oracle intents, and (3) the verifier's F2P gates, which are the strongest available signal for 'done'. The bug is structurally two-part: a basic-mode gate that dropped the preset's phase_config (goal_1, the dominant core) and a missing preset-id propagation into the task params (goal_2, near-equal core); these two cores carry 0.82 combined, mirroring the 0.82 reward the better model attempts reached and reflecting that the verifier's weighted F2P mass concentrates on exactly these two behaviors. goal_3 captures the multi-turn UX corrections (stay in basic mode visually, settings visible in advanced) which the deterministic verifier cannot fully check but which are explicit, verbatim user asks — weighted secondary. The Turn-4 'push to github' intent is a workflow/process action and is deliberately NOT a completeness goal. Weights intentionally do NOT mirror the manifest's per-gate F2P weights one-to-one: the manifest splits the data-flow into ~8 fine gates plus upstream duplicates; this rubric collapses them into two broad behavioral cores so any behaviorally-equivalent implementation passes regardless of how many files or fields it touches. goal_0 is the zero-weight anti-gaming floor calibrated against the empty/diff-pad submissions seen here and the type/destructure-only edits that Gate 4 explicitly rejects. Weighted goals sum to 1.00 (0.42 + 0.40 + 0.18).
